content.full_text:
. -; STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
< FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 1 428 ,J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
1 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Richard Mendelsohn 
Member, Public Art Advisory Committee 
Town of Windsor 
9248 Eastside Road 
Healdsburg, CA 95448 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-154 
Dear Mr. Mendelsohn: 
August 31, 20 I 6 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political 
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice. 
(111 re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit you from taking part in the governmental decision to recommend one 
of three alternative design options to the Town Council for the renovation of the water fountain 
located in the Windsor Town Green community park given that you own a commercial office 
building, which you rent out to a solar energy company, located near the water fountain? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in that decision because it would not 
have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on your financial interests. 
FACTS 
The Town of Windsor recently entered into an agreement with an architectural firm to 
provide design services for the renovation of the Town's water fountain located in the Windsor 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
-references arc 10 the Gmernmcnt Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Pructicci, 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references arc 10 Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Rl!gulatiol)s. unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-154 
Page No. 2 
Town Green, a 4.5-acre community park located in the Town's Old Downtown. The water fountain 
runs the length of McClelland Drive and is currently run by mechanical equipment housed 
underground. Although the renovation will include a new !,tructural and artistic design for the 
fountain, the foremost priority for the renovation is to provide Town maintenance staff with a safe, 
accessible, above-ground way to regularly service the fountain's mechanical equipment. 
The Town's Public Art Advisory Committee ("PAAC") will soon consider, and receive 
public input regarding, three alternative design options for the renovation of the fountain provided 
by the firm, and will recommend one of those options to the Town Council. The PAAC is 
composed of one Town Councilmember, one Town Parks and Recreation Commissioner, and five 
citizens appointed at large by the Town Council for four-year terms. The Town considers the PAAC 
to be a decision-making body and treats PAAC members as public officials. 
You are a citizen member of the PAAC, appointed at large by the Town Council. You own a 
commercial office building located approximately 450 feet from the center of the Windsor Town 
Green, where the fountain is located. Your commercial office building has a single tenant, a solar 
energy company. You receive rental income from the solar energy company, but do not operate any 
business out of the commercial office building. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a 
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. 
Section 87103 also identifie!, the financial interests that may give rise to a conflict of interest under 
the Act, and of those interests, the facts presented and the decision at issue implicate the following: 
• 
Any interest in a busine!is entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trm,tee, 
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). You have an interest in 
your business renting out your commercial office building. 
• 
Any interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or 
more. (Section 87103(b).) You have a real-property interest in your commercial office building. 
• 
Any interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which aggregates 
to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) You have a source­
of-income interest in your business and in your sole tenant, the solar energy company. 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family, 
also known as the "personal financial effects" Rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18702.5.) An 
official always has an interest in his or her own personal finances. 


Foreseeability and Materiality 
File No. A-16-154 
Page No. 3 
For a financial interest not "explicitly involved"1 in the decision at issue, the financial effect 
of the decision on an official• s interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a real is tic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 1870l(b).) 
Regulation 18702.2 sets forth the materiality standards for a decision's effect on an 
official's real property interest, and Regulation 18702.5 provides the materiality standards for a 
personal financial effect. Generally, when a decision affects more than one of an official's financial 
interests, the effect on each interest must be considered. In this instance, however, Regulation 
I 8702.2(a)( 11 ), the materiality standard applicable to your real property interest, and Regulation 
l 8702.5(c), the materiality standard applicable to your interest in your personal finances, both 
instead require the application of Regulation 18702.1, the regulation that provides the materiality 
standards applicable to an official's business interest. 
Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards for a decision's effect on an 
official's interest in a source of income. You have an interest in your commercial real property 
rental business and in your tenant, the solar energy company, as sources of income. And Regulation 
18702.3(a)(4) provides that the materiality of a decision's effect on a business entity that is a source 
of income to the official is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1. 
Thus, whether the effects of the decision at issue on your business or the solar energy 
company arc material is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1. 
Regulation 18702.1 (b) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision· s effect on 
an official's interest in a business entity that is not explicitly involved in the decision. Regulation 
18702.1 (b) provides that such an effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information 
would find it reasonably foreseeable that the effect would contribute to a change in the value of the 
business entity. Regulation 18702. I (b) also provides examples of decisions that may have a 
material effect on a business entity, including the example of a decision that would "[m]ake 
improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment projects, traffic/road 
improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount 
of business the business entity receives." (Regulation 18702.1 (b )( 4 ).) 
Although the recommendation of one of the three designs for the renovation of the water 
fountain may lead to an improvement to the aesthetics of the neighborhood surrounding your 
commercial office building, there is no indication from the facts presented that the mere renovation 
of the existing water fountain would likely result in any additional business for. or contribute to a 
u An oflicial's financial interest is explicitly involved in a governmental dec1s1on if the interest 1s a named 
party in. or suhject of. the decision. A financial intere!.t is the subject of a proceeding tf the decision inrnl\es the 
i!.suance, renewal. approval. denial. or revocation of any license. permit. or other entitlement to. or contract with. the 
financial interest. or if the decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation I 8702.2(a)( I )-(6). 
(Regulation 1870 l(a).) 


File No. A-16-15..J 
Page No. 4 
change in the value of, either business.1 Therefore, we find that the renovation of the water fountain 
would not foreseeably change the value of your business or the solar energy company. 
Accordingly, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit you from taking part in the decision 
to recommend one of three alternative design options to the Town Council for the renovation of the 
water fountain. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Couns 
 
By: 
Matthew F. Chnsty 
Counsel, Legal Division 
J Regulation I 8702(h) cautions: "Notwithstanding Regulation!> 18702.1 through 18702.5. the linanc1al effect of 
a governmental decision is not material if it is nominal. inconsequential. or insignificant."

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit you from taking part in the governmental decision to recommend one 
of three alternative design options to the Town Council for the renovation of the water fountain 
located in the Windsor Town Green community park given that you own a commercial office 
building, which you rent out to a solar energy company, located near the water fountain?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in that decision because it would not 
have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on your financial interests.

QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit you from taking part in the governmental decision to recommend one 
of three alternative design options to the Town Council for the renovation of the water fountain 
located in the Windsor Town Green community park given that you own a commercial office 
building, which you rent out to a solar energy company, located near the water fountain?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in that decision because it would not 
have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on your financial interests.
